LAW AND MOTION 302, DEFENDANT COMMUNITY HOUSING PARTNERSHIP'S DEMURRER TO THE SECOND CAUSE OF ACTION FOR VIOLATION OF THE ELDER ABUSE AND DEPENDENT ADULT CIVIL PROTECTION ACT IS SUSTAINED WITH TWENTY DAYS LEAVE TO AMEND. THE COMPLAINT DOES NOT SUFFICIENTLY ALLEGE THAT THIS DEFENDANT'S ALLEGED FAILURE TO MAKE WELFARE CHECKS ON THE DECEDENT WAS DONE MALICIOUSLY, OPPRESSIVELY OR WITH CONSCIOUS DISREGARD OF THE HIGH PROBABILITY THAT DECEDENT WOULD DIE OR SUFFER OTHER HARM. (CARTER V. PRIME HEALTHCARE PARADISE VALLEY LLC (2011) 198 CAL. APP. 4TH 396, 406-7). NOR DOES THE COMPLAINT SUFFICIENTLY ALLEGE A CAUSAL LINK BETWEEN THE ALLEGED FAILURE TO MAKE THE WELFARE CHECKS AND DECEDENT'S DEATH OR OTHER HARM. (ID. AT 407). IF THEY CAN DO SO IN GOOD FAITH, PLAINTIFFS ARE GIVEN LEAVE TO CORRECT THESE DEFICIENCIES. ORDER SIGNED IN OPEN COURT. JUDGE: JUDGE KAHN. CLERK: ROSIE NOGUERA. NOT REPORTED. (302/HK)

LAW AND MOTION 302, DEFENDANT COMMUNITY HOUSING PARTNERSHIP'S MOTION TO STRIKE THE PUNITIVE DAMAGES AND ATTORNEY'S ALLEGATIONS IN THE COMPLAINT IS GRANTED WITH TWENTY DAYS LEAVE TO AMEND. AS STATED IN THE RULING ON THIS DEFENDANT'S DEMURRER, THE COMPLAINT INSUFFICIENTLY ALLEGES LIABILITY UNDER THE ELDER ABUSE AND DEPENDENT ADULT CIVIL PROTECTION ACT AND PLAINTIFFS ARE GRANTED LEAVE TO CORRECT SUCH DEFICIENCIES IF THEY CAN DO SO IN GOOD FAITH. ORDER SIGNED IN OPEN COURT. JUDGE: JUDGE KAHN. CLERK: ROSIE NOGUERA. NOT REPORTED. (302/HK)

MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT Or, In The Alternative,, Summary Adjudication
Off Calendar Nov-02-2017 Denied
MINI MINUTES: Appearances:
Sedric Chappill, Esq. 510-575-7508 for Plaintiff.
B. Thomas French, Esq. 415-28809800 of Hassard Bonnington for Defendant the Regents of UC.

RULING - A R G U E D. The Court shares his views. Defendant argues common knowledge exception of expert witness, question of professional negligence, causation, disputed facts, ostensible agency issue. Plaintiff responds/argues misstatements. The Court having reviewed the pleadings and having heard oral adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

DEFENDANT THE REGENTS OF THE UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA's MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT Or, In The Alternative,, Summary Adjudication.

Defendant The Regents of the University of California's motion for summary judgment and alternate motion for summary adjudication are denied in their entirety. There is a triable issue whether The Regents breached its standard of care. This case does not require expert testimony. The common knowledge doctrine applies because a lay person could determine whether decedent needed assistance. Plaintiffs allege that The Regents was directly negligent by failing to perform a wellness check on May 19, 2015. (Regents' Fact 18.) There is a triable issue whether The Regents agreed to make wellness checks and breached its duty of care by failing to do so on May 19, 2015. Plaintiffs presented evidence that a representative from CHP and The Regents agreed to provide wellness checks. (David Chappill Depo., 191:24-192:10; Otten Depo. 40:19-41:4; 64:21-65:9.) There is a triable issue whether The Regents had notice on May 19, 2015 that decedent needed help. On May 19, 2015, Ms. Gregory notified Alex and Dorothy that there were concerns about decedent. (Gregory Depo., 22:3-21; 56:3-9.) Alex was an employee of The Regents. The Regents fails to satisfy its burden of production on its causation argument. The Regents merely shows that the medical examiner attributed the death to natural causes. There is an insufficient showing that The Regents' intervention would not have helped. Dr. Zier's opinion that there is no causation is based on the assumption that The Regents did not receive notice on May 19, 2015 which, as noted above, is disputed. The Regents fails to satisfy its burden of production on the issues of ostensible agency and whether The Regents can be vicariously liable for CHP's alleged negligence. The evidence that The Regents cites for its fact 42 is equivocal and merely shows that the decedent's relatives understood that CHP and The Regents worked together. The evidence does not show that ostensible agency "cannot be established" within the meaning of CCP 437c. Moreover, there is a triable issue regarding ostensible agency. The existence of an agency relationship is a question of fact. (Senens v. Haas (1955) 45 Cal.2d 811, 831.) The evidence presented by plaintiffs shows that The Regents made statements to plaintiffs which support the existence of an agency relationship. (David Chappill Depo., 189:16-21; Otten Depo., 95:4-10; 39:21-41:4; David Chappill Dec., par. 5.)(as fully reported in the reporter's transcript)

Off Calendar Oct-11-2017 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
No appearance.

RULING: There being no appearance or objection, the Court adopted its tentative ruling on DEFENDANT COMMUNITY HOUSING PARTNERSHIP'S MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT Or, Alternatively Summary Adjudication, Of Issues as follows:

Defendant Community Housing Partnership's motion for summary judgment is denied and its alternate motion for summary adjudication is granted as to plaintiff David Chappill, Sr.'s claim for negligent infliction of emotional distress and denied as to the claims for survival (negligence) and wrongful death. CHP has not provided sufficient evidence to meet its summary judgment/adjudication burden to show that it owed no duty to Lamar Chappill or that the Medical Examiner's determination precludes liability against it. CHP's separate statement does not contain sufficient facts showing that CHP lacked information on May 19, 2015 to put it on notice that it should enter Lamar Chappill's unit, call 911 or take some other step to ensure the wellbeing of Lamar Chappill. Nor does CHP's separate statement contain sufficient facts showing that the Medical Examiner's cause of death determination relieved CHP of any duty it had to ensure the wellbeing of Lamar Chappill on May 19, 2015. David Chappill's claim for negligent infliction of emotional distress fails. David Chappill purports to allege a direct negligent infliction of emotional distress claim rather than a bystander claim. However, David Chappill's allegations (see, e.g., pars. 52-54 and 60 of the Third Amended Complaint) are insufficient to show that CHP assumed or owed a duty of care to him. David Chappill's citation to Molien v. Kaiser Foundation Hospitals (1980) 27 Cal.3d 916 is off point. Molien held that a hospital and a doctor owed a duty directly to the husband of a patient, who had been diagnosed incorrectly by the doctor as having syphilis and had been told to so advise her husband in order that he could receive testing and, if necessary, treatment. The court reasoned that the risk of harm to the husband was reasonably foreseeable and that the alleged tortious conduct of the defendant was directed to him as well as to his wife. There are no similar facts alleged in this case and the California Supreme Court has stated that "[s]ince Molien was decided, we have made clear that despite its broad references to foreseeability, the opinion should be read as basing the defendant doctor's direct-victim liability only upon his assumption of a direct duty toward the husband." (Huggins v. Longs Drug Stores California, Inc. (1993) 6 Cal.4th 124, 130).

Counsel for plaintiff to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the Court's ruling and submit it in compliance with CRC 3.1312. Judge: Harold Kahn; Clerk: Gina Gonzales; Reporter: Reyes Hunter CSR #6576 email: rhunter@sftc.org.

MINI MINUTES: Appearances: Jami Tencati of Hassard Bonnington LLP telephone number 415-288-9800 for Defendant The Regents of The University of California.
No other appearance was made by any Party, Counsel or Interested Person.

The Court confirmed its tentative ruling. Defendant The Regents of The University of California's demurrer to the second cause of action for elder abuse and the fourth cause of action for negligent infliction of emotional distress in the third amended complaint is sustained without leave to amend as to both causes of action. Plaintiffs fail to state a claim for elder abuse. In Carter v. Prime Healthcare Paradise Valley, LLC, (2011) 198 Cal.App.4th 396, 406-407, the court explains that: "The plaintiff must allege ' facts establishing that the defendant: (1) had responsibility for meeting the basic needs of the elder or dependent adult, such as nutrition, hydration, hygiene or medical care; (2) knew of conditions that made the elder or dependent adult unable to provide for his or her own basic needs [citation]; and (3) denied or withheld goods or services necessary to meet the elder or dependent adult's basic needs, either with knowledge that injury was substantially certain to befall the elder or dependent adult (if the plaintiff alleges oppression, fraud or malice) or with conscious disregard of the high probability of such injury (if the plaintiff alleges recklessness)." (Internal citation omitted). Plaintiffs do not allege sufficient facts showing that The Regents withheld necessary services with a conscious disregard for decedent's safety. Paragraphs 8 and 28(a) of the third amended complaint allege that the police informed The Regents that decedent was "OK" on May 18, 2015 and paragraphs 32 (h) and (i) allege that The Regents' agent checked on decedent on May 19, 2016 and heard shuffling in his apartment and concluded that decedent was "OK." These allegations indicate that The Regents responded to decedent. Plaintiff does not allege conduct sufficiently egregious to constitute neglect (or any other form of abuse) within the meaning of the Elder Abuse Act. The NIED direct victim claim by plaintiff David Chappill, Sr. also lacks sufficient facts to state a viable claim. Mr. Chappill's allegations (see, e.g., paragraphs. 52-54) are insufficient to show that The Regents assumed or owed a duty of care to him. Molien v. Kaiser Foundation Hospitals (1980) 27 Cal.3d 916 is readily distinguishable. In Molien the court held that a hospital and a doctor owed a duty directly to the husband of a patient, who had been diagnosed incorrectly by the doctor as having syphilis and had been told to so advise her husband in order that he could receive testing and, if necessary, treatment. The Molien court reasoned that the risk of harm to the husband was reasonably foreseeable and that the alleged tortious conduct of the defendant was directed to him as well as to his wife. There are no such facts in this case and "[s]ince Molien was decided, we have made clear that despite its broad references to foreseeability, the opinion should be read as basing the defendant doctor's direct-victim liability only upon his assumption of a direct duty toward the husband." Huggins v. Longs Drug Stores California, Inc. (1993) 6 Cal.4th 124, 130; see also Ochoa v. Superior Court (1985) 39 Cal.3d 159 (plaintiff mother could recover emotional distress damages as a bystander for observing defendant withhold care from her son but she could not recover as a direct victim). Order signed in open Court.

APPEARANCES - ATTY. B. THOMAS FRENCH OF HASSARD BONNINGTON LLP, TEL. NO. 1-415-288-9800 APPEARING FOR THE REGENTS OF THE UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA;
NO OTHER APPEARANCES ON THIS MATTER.

RULING - NOTICE WAS GIVEN TO CONTEST THE TENTATIVE RULING. THE MATTER WAS ARGUED. The court modified its tentative ruling and ruled as follows: DEFENDANT THE REGENTS OF THE UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA'S DEMURRER TO 1ST AMENDED COMPLAINT - The Regents' demurrer to the all four claims in the first amended complaint is overruled as to the first and third causes of action for wrongful death and general negligence and sustained with twenty days leave to amend as to the second and fourth causes of action for violation of the dependent adult statute and negligent infliction of emotional distress. Liberally construed, the facts alleged in the FAC adequately allege the capacity of decedent's estate to sue for the first three claims and adequately alleges claims for wrongful death and general negligence. The second cause of action insufficiently alleges facts that the Regents' alleged failure to make welfare checks was done maliciously, oppressively or with conscious disregard of the high probability that decedent would die or be harmed. (Carter v. Prime Healthcare Paradise Valley, LLC (2011) 198 Cal. App. 4th 396, 406-7). Nor does the FAC sufficiently allege a causal link between the failure to make welfare checks and decedent's death. The fourth cause of action does not allege facts showing that the Regents owed a duty of care to David Chappill, Sr. to support an NIED claim. If they can do so in good faith, plaintiffs are granted leave to correct the deficiencies in their allegations in the second and fourth causes of action. Order signed in open court.
